meta.title: Demonstrate understanding of mechanics
meta.purpose: This achievement standard involves demonstrating understanding of mechanics.

grade_descriptors.N1: Evidence does not reach the minimum threshold for Achieved-level indicators.
grade_descriptors.N2: Some Achieved-level indicators are met, but not enough to satisfy all required Achieved criteria.
grade_descriptors.A3: All Achieved-level indicators are met; no Merit-level indicator meets the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.A4: All Achieved indicators are met and at least one Merit indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.M5: All Achieved indicators are met and the Merit threshold is reached; no Excellence-level indicator meets the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.M6: All Achieved indicators are met, all Merit indicators are met, and at least one Excellence indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.E7: All Achieved and Merit indicators are met and the Excellence threshold is reached, but not all Excellence indicators are met.
grade_descriptors.E8: All Achieved, Merit, and Excellence indicators are met.

key_terms.definition: the study of motion, forces, and energy and how they interact
the product of mass and velocity of an object (p = mv)
when all forces and torques on an object are balanced
the force that keeps an object moving in a circle (Fc = mv²/r)

omis.description: States or selects a relevant mechanics principle, law, or correct formula for the situation.

positive_signals: Explicit naming of a specific physics law (e.g., 'Newton's second law')
negative_signals: Generic mention of 'a law' without specification
Formula stated but not relevant to the situation
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Note that simply mentioning 'a law' or 'a formula' is insufficient; the specific, correct one must be explicitly named or written.

success_examples: The law of conservation of momentum applies.

omis.description: Accurately labels diagrams or graphs with quantities (e.g., forces, velocity, axes).

positive_signals: Labels matching the physics quantity (e.g., 'F' for force arrows)
Correct placement of labels on the diagram
Axes labeled with quantity and units (e.g., 'velocity (m/s)')
negative_signals: Incorrect labels (e.g., wrong symbol or quantity)
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Note that labels must correspond exactly to the relevant physics quantity and be placed correctly; incorrect or misplaced labels do not meet the criteria.

omis.description: Performs a straightforward calculation using a mechanics formula to solve for a single variable.

negative_signals: Using an unrelated or incorrect equation
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Note that the calculation must be straightforward using the correct formula with proper substitution and units; guessing or unrelated equations do not count.

omis.description: Links a mechanics principle or formula to the described situation, showing how it applies.

positive_signals: Principle connected to the specific situation (e.g., 'gravity causes acceleration')
Formula applied with explanation (e.g., 'F = ma applies here')
Shows how the concept fits the context
negative_signals: Just reciting the principle without connection
No explanation of how it applies
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Note that merely stating the principle or formula is insufficient; it must be linked and shown how it applies to the specific situation.

success_examples: Because only gravity acts, the ball accelerates at 9.8 m/s².

omis.description: Gives a reason why a principle, concept, or law is relevant to the situation.

positive_signals: Clear cause-effect reasoning (e.g., 'no external forces, so momentum conserved')
Explanation of relevance to the situation
negative_signals: Vague statements like 'because it is needed'
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Note that stating 'because it is needed' or similar vagueness is insufficient; a clear cause-effect relationship or specific reasoning must be demonstrated.

success_examples: Conservation of momentum applies because there are no external forces.

omis.description: Correctly solves a multi-step or non-obvious calculation requiring two or more physics principles.

positive_signals: Logical progression from one step to the next
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Note that every step must be shown and logically follow using correct physics; skipping steps or using wrong principles invalidates the solution.

success_examples: Calculate acceleration, then use it to find final velocity.

omis.description: Explains the relationship between two or more physical quantities in context.

positive_signals: Quantities linked with cause-effect (e.g., 'force causes acceleration')
Describes how one affects the other
negative_signals: No description of how they relate
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Note that merely listing the quantities is insufficient; the explanation must link them and describe how one affects the other in the given context.

omis.description: Correctly interprets or explains features of a graph or vector diagram in relation to the physical situation.

positive_signals: Graph features interpreted in physics context (e.g., 'slope = acceleration')
Explanation of what features mean physically
negative_signals: Just describing shape without physics context
Failing to connect to the situation
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Note that merely describing the shape or features of the graph/diagram is insufficient; it must be interpreted in relation to the physical situation.

success_examples: The slope of the velocity-time graph gives acceleration.

omis.description: Shows how two or more mechanics concepts connect or interact across different contexts.

negative_signals: No explanation of how they interact
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Note that merely mentioning two or more concepts is insufficient; they must be clearly linked with explanation of their interaction or mutual influence.

success_examples: Momentum conservation and energy loss both explain the car crash outcome.

omis.description: Provides a comprehensive, accurate, and concise written explanation of a mechanics principle or process.

negative_signals: Inclusion of errors or irrelevant details
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Note that the explanation must be comprehensive yet concise, accurate, and clear, covering all key points without unnecessary detail or factual errors.

success_examples: Work is the product of force and distance moved in the direction of the force.

omis.description: Applies physics knowledge accurately to an unfamiliar or complex context.

positive_signals: Physics principles applied to new context
Addresses specific aspects of the unfamiliar situation
negative_signals: Ignoring unique aspects of the situation
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Note that merely repeating memorized facts is insufficient; the knowledge must be accurately transferred and applied to address the specifics of the unfamiliar or complex context.

success_examples: Explaining projectile motion on a different planet.
